title: Projects
p1-descriptor:
Film and TV editors spend hours searching for temp music — sometimes just to score 20 seconds of a scene. SceneSync lets you drop in a reference track and get royalty-free alternatives that work the same way acoustically, with a plain-language explanation of why each match fits.

The core question driving it: what in the audio signal produces that feeling? Not 'find something similar' — but 'here's the acoustic DNA of your reference, and here's why these suggestions share it.'

SceneSync extracts a 90-value acoustic fingerprint via Meyda (energy, brightness, timbral texture, harmonic content) and compares it against a curated library of 243 royalty-free tracks using cosine similarity. An LLM explanation layer translates the signal math into plain language — describing what the track is doing acoustically and why each match serves the same scene need. Everything runs client-side: no server, no uploads, analysis and history persisted locally in IndexedDB.

I started this as a scene classifier — predicting Action / Romance / Drama / Tension from audio features. At 50% accuracy on 20 tracks it was a coin flip, and more importantly it wasn't answering the right question. The pivot to acoustic similarity search came from realizing the interesting problem wasn't prediction, it was explanation. I'm coming at this as a former music teacher who grew up obsessed with how music shapes scenes — the slapstick chaos of Tom and Jerry, the emotional precision of Studio Ghibli and Pixar scores. Building this to understand whether those patterns can be surfaced and articulated.
p1-stack: React, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, Web Audio API, Web Workers, Meyda, Node.js, Python, IndexedDB, Cloudflare R2, DeepSeek API
p2-descriptor: In 2025, I led a 9-month digital transformation for Kuang-Hwa Vocational Senior High School — an institution my grandfather founded in 1951. The school needed a digital presence that matched its 70-year legacy and reflected its evolving mission. I project-managed a complete overhaul: migrating from a legacy system to WordPress/Elementor on Google Cloud Platform, coordinating with designer Genie's team, and working across departments (admissions, academics, alumni relations) to modernize how the school presents itself. The challenge wasn't just technical — it was navigating institutional culture, balancing stakeholder priorities, and building something sustainable for a team that would maintain it long after I stepped away. The site launched in September 2025. It addressed each department's needs and redefined what a school website could be—shifting from a passive information repository to an active tool for connecting the community.
p2-stack: WordPress, Elementor, Google Cloud Platform, Project Management
p3-descriptor: I built PollyGlot after trips to Italy and Spain where Duolingo failed me. I'd spent weeks learning phrases I'd never actually use in conversation — meanwhile, I couldn't figure out how to ask a local for restaurant recommendations without sounding either too formal or accidentally rude. Google Translate gives you words, but it doesn't help you navigate tone. PollyGlot lets you specify context before translating across 6 tones — formal, friendly, slang, and more. Need to ask a street vendor about their produce? Choose friendly. Writing an email to a professor? Formal. Trying to joke with your Airbnb host? Slang. It supports 16 languages including Klingon and Esperanto (because some experiments exist just for fun). The app uses OpenAI's API for translation and tone adjustment. It's not replacing human translators — cultural nuance is hard — but it bridges the gap between direct translation and actual conversation.
p3-stack: React, Node.js, Express, OpenAI API, CSS
p4-descriptor: Designed and built a single-page application where users can search for movies and nominate their top five favorites. Leveraged OMDB API to provide real-time movie data and React for an optimized interactive experience.
p4-stack: React, JavaScript, CSS, HTML, OMDB API
